beats.narration: Light bends every time it changes medium. Snell's law tells you exactly how much — and it runs every optic you carry.

payload.title: WHY LIGHT BENDS
payload.sub: Snell's law is the working principle behind every inspection optic.

beats.narration: Light slows entering denser glass, so it bends toward the normal. Exiting into faster air, it bends away. Snell's law pins this down.

payload.heading: Refraction at an air-glass boundary
payload.note: N1 × SIN θ1 = N2 × SIN θ2

beats.narration: That's why a pin gauge looks bent in water, and a flaw under a water film isn't where it appears to be.

chips.t: Pin gauge looks bent in water
Flaw under water isn't where it appears

beats.narration: Every lens, prism, and borescope window works the same way — just glass arranged to bend light on purpose.
Change the medium, change the speed, change the direction — that is all a lens ever does.

payload.sub: That is all a lens ever does.
